content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

July 14, 2016

Steven T. Mattas
City Attorney
Town of Los Altos Hills
575 Market Street, Suite 2080
San Francisco, California 94105

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-16-113

Dear Mr. Mattas:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf the Town of Los Altos Hills Mayor
John Harpootlian, Vice Mayor Gary Waldeck, and City Councilmembers John Radford and Roger
Spreen (hereafter, "Councilmember," respectively, or "Councilmembers," collectively) regarding
the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ This letter is based on
the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission ("Commission") does not act as a
finder of fact when it renders advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 1.) Please note that we
are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other
general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090.

QUESTION

Does the Act prohibit Councilmembers Harpootlian, Waldeck, Spreen, or Radford from
taking part in the governmental decision on whether to amend the Town's Wildland Urban Interface
("WUI") map?

CONCLUSION

The Act does not prohibit Councilmember Radford from taking part in the decision on
whether to amend the WUI map. Although the Act prohibits Councilmembers Harpootlian,
Waldeck, and Spreen from taking part in that decision, the Town may invoke the "legally required
participation" exception to form a quorum of councilmembers with respect to the decision, as
specified in Regulation 18705.

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-16-113
Page No. 2

FACTS

You are the City Attorney for the Town of Los Altos Hills, and the authorized representative
of Councilmembers Harpootlian, Waldeck, Spreen, and Radford.

In 2011, the Town adopted a Wildland Urban Interface map ("WUI map"), indicating the
area of the Town at increased risk from wildfires (the "WUI area"), and imposing more stringent
and costly building requirements on residential parcels within the WUI area to protect them from
intensified fire hazards. At the time, the Town placed 2,308 of its 3,016 residential parcels within
the WUI area, and those parcels remain there today, as the Town has not updated the WUI map.

In mid-2015, the Town's Planning Commission formed a Subcommittee to consider
potential amendments to the WUI map to modify the WUI area. The Subcommittee could not
decide which of the potential amendments to the WUI map to recommend, so it presented three
options to the Planning Commission for its consideration. On September 3, 2015, the Planning
Commission discussed the following three options for amending the WUI map:

• Option 1: Eliminate the existing WUI map in its entirety. This option would remove all
2,308 residential parcels currently from the WUI area, thereby affecting 77 percent of all the
residential parcels in Town.

• Option 2: Move the WUI area boundary back to Interstate 280. Under this option, the
eastern boundary of the WUI area would be moved west to Interstate 280. This option would
affect approximately 600 residential parcels, or nearly 20 percent of all those in Town, by either
adding or removing them from the WUI area.

• Option 3: Move the WUI area boundary back beyond Interstate 280. This option would
move the eastern boundary of the WUI area west beyond Interstate 280, and remove
approximately 1,000 residential parcels, or 33 percent of all those in Town, from the WUI area.

After discussion, the Planning Commission voted in favor of presenting all three options for
amending the WUI map to the City Council, along with the following fourth option:

• Option 4: Move the WUI area boundary generally back to Interstate 280 but include
additional properties in the northernmost portion of Town. This option would move the
eastern boundary of the WUI area west to Interstate 280, except in the northernmost portion of
Town, where the WUI area would include some properties east of Interstate 280. This option
would affect approximately 500 residential parcels, or less than 17 percent of all those in Town,
by removing them from the WUI area.

The City Council will soon consider all four options and determine whether to amend the
Town's WUI map.

Councilmembers Harpootlian, Waldeck, and Spreen own their respective primary residences
within the existing WUI area, and Councilmember Radford owns his primary residence outside the
WUI area but within 500 feet of the existing WUI area boundary. If the City Council decides to
eliminate the WUI map in its entirety (Option 1), Councilmembers Harpootlian, Waldeck, and

File No. A-16-113
Page No. 3

Spreen's respective residences would be removed from the WUI area. If the City Council decides to
adopt any of the other three options for the amendment of the WUI map, Councilmembers
Harpootlian and Waldeck's respective residences would be removed from the WUI area, but
Councilmember Spreen's residence would remain within. Under any of the four options to amend
the WUI map, Councilmember Radford's residence would remain outside the WUI area.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits a public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise
using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a
financial interest. Under Section 87103(b), an official has a financial interest in a decision within
the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect on any real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest worth
$2,000 or more.

Councilmembers Harpootlian, Waldeck, and Spreen own their respective primary
residences, all of which are currently within the WUI area, and Councilmember Radford owns his
primary residence, located within 500 feet of the border of the existing WUI area. Based on the
facts presented, these Councilmembers' only interests implicated by the decision on whether to
amend the WUI map are their respective real property interests in their residences.

As an initial matter, you ask about segmenting the five options being presented to the city
council. While in some cases, "large, complex decisions may be segmented into separate decisions
so that even if an official has a disqualifying interest in one component of the series of decisions"
(emphasis added) where a decision in which an official has a disqualifying interest is "inextricably
interrelated" to other decisions, the official will be required to disqualify from all the linked
decisions. Regulation 18706(b) provides that "decisions are 'inextricably interrelated' when the
result of one decision will effectively determine, affirm, nullify, or alter the result of another
decision."

Your question presents a single decision, what to do with the existing WUI map. All the
options to amend the WUI map are "inextricably interrelated" to this single decision.² The result of
a decision on one of the options would effectively determine, affirm, nullify, or alter the result of
the decisions on the other options. Therefore, the decision on whether to amend the WUI map is not
capable of segmentation.

² See Pittman Advice Letter, No. A-14-039 (decisions on whether to include particular properties on the
housing inventory list of the Housing Element of the city's General Plan were inextricably interrelated because the
decision to include one property could affect the decision on another); Hildreth Advice Letter, No. A-10-031 (decisions
relating to the boundaries of a proposed community plan that could determine the inclusion or exclusion of a particular
property were inextricably interrelated); Campos Advice Letter, No. I-06-072 (decisions to rezone separate parcels to
meet a high-density housing requirement applicable to all the parcels that could affect the allotment of the burden
between those parcels were inextricably interrelated.)

File No. A-16-113
Page No. 4

Foreseeability & Materiality: Councilmembers Harpootlian, Waldeck, and Spreen

A governmental decision's financial effect on a public official's financial interest is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the interest is a named party in, or the subject of, the decision. (Regulation 18701(a).) An official's financial interest is a named party in, or subject of, a decision if, among other things, the decision affects a real property interest described in Regulation 18702.2(a)(1)-(6). (Ibid.)

Regulation 18702.2(a)(2) provides that the reasonably foreseeable effect of a governmental decision on an official's real property interest is material whenever the decision determines the parcel's inclusion or exclusion from any boundaries, other than boundaries for elective purposes.

The City Council will soon consider whether to amend the WUI map, and that decision will determine whether Councilmembers Harpootlian, Waldeck, and Spreen's respective real property interests in their residences will be included within, or excluded from, the amended WUI area. Therefore, the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on these Councilmembers' respective real property interests.

Foreseeability & Materiality: Councilmember Radford

If a public official's financial interest is not explicitly involved in a governmental decision, the financial effect of the decision is reasonably foreseeable if the effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. (Regulation 18701(b).)

A governmental decision's financial effect is material if the decision affects the value of real property located within 500 feet of the property line of the official's real property, unless the Commission finds that there are sufficient facts to indicate there will be no reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on the official's real property interest. (Regulation 18702.2(a)(11).)

Councilmember Radford's residence is outside the existing WUI area, and therefore, his residence currently is not subject to the more stringent and costly building requirements imposed on parcels within the WUI area. Under any of the four options to amend the WUI map under consideration, Councilmember Radford's residence would remain outside the WUI area. Although the decision at issue will determine whether parcels within 500 feet of Councilmember Radford's residence are included within, or excluded from, the WUI area, the inclusion or exclusion of those parcels will not affect Councilmember Radford's residence. Therefore, we find that there are sufficient facts to indicate that the decision at issue will not have a reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on Councilmember Radford's real property interest in his residence. Accordingly, the Act does not prohibit Councilmember Radford from taking part in the decision.

Public Generally Exception

Section 87103 prohibits an official from taking part in a decision only if the effect of the decision on the official's interest is distinguishable from the effect on the public generally. Commonly referred to as the "public generally" exception, Regulation 18703(a) sets forth the general rule:

File No. A-16-113
Page No. 5

"A governmental decision's financial effect on a public official's financial interest is indistinguishable from its effect on the public generally if the official establishes that a significant segment of the public is affected and the effect on his or her financial interest is not unique compared to the effect on the significant segment."

For purposes of the "public generally" exception, a "significant segment of the public" consists of at least 25 percent of (1) the businesses or non-profit entities; (2) the real property, commercial real property, or residential real property; or (3) the individuals within the official's jurisdiction. (Regulation 18703(b).) With respect to the decision at issue, the "significant segment of the public" is 25 percent of the residential real property within the Town.

In making the determination on whether to amend the WUI map, the City Council will consider four options for its potential amendment. Option 1, to eliminate the WUI map in its entirety, would affect 77 percent of all residential parcels in Town, and Option 3, to move the WUI boundary back beyond Interstate 280, would affect 33 percent of all residential parcels. However, Option 2, to move the boundary back to Interstate 280, would only affect less than 20 percent of all residential parcels in Town, and Option 4, to move the boundary generally back to Interstate 280 but include additional properties in the northernmost portion of Town, would affect less than 17 percent of all residential parcels. Therefore, the "public generally" exception does not apply because the decision on whether to amend the WUI map may not affect a "significant segment of the public."

Accordingly, the Act prohibits Councilmembers Harpootlian, Waldeck, and Spreen from taking part in the decision at issue because that decision could have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on their respective real property interests in their residences distinguishable from its effect on the public generally.

Legally Required Participation Exception

Under the "legally required participation" exception set forth in Section 87101 and Regulation 18705, a public official may make or participate in the making of a governmental decision despite being financially interested in the decision if the official's participation is legally required for the action or decision to be made.

Regulation 18705(c) requires the "legally required participation" exception to be construed narrowly. The exception cannot be invoked to allow a financially interested official to participate in a decision if a quorum³ can be convened of other members of the agency who are not disqualified, and participation is required to be limited to the smallest number of officials with a disqualifying interest that are "legally required" to make the decision. (Regulation 18705(c)(2)-(3).)

Because the Town's City Council is made up of five councilmembers, at least three will be necessary to form a quorum to decide whether to amend the WUI map. Councilmembers Harpootlian, Waldeck, and Spreen are disqualified from taking part in the decision pursuant to

³ As is pertinent to the facts presented, Regulation 18705(d) defines a "quorum" as "the minimum number of members required to conduct business."

File No. A-16-113
Page No. 6

Section 87100, leaving less than a quorum of councilmembers available to make that determination. Therefore, one of those disqualified Councilmembers will be "legally required" to take part in that decision in order to conduct business, and the Town may invoke the exception.

Accordingly, the Town is authorized to use a random means of selection to select a single councilmember from among Councilmembers Harpootlian, Waldeck, and Spreen to form a quorum with respect to the decision at issue. (See Regulation 18705(c)(3).) When one of those councilmembers is selected, that councilmember is selected for the duration of the proceedings in all related matters until that councilmember's participation is no longer legally required, or the need for invoking the exception no longer exists. (Ibid.) The selected councilmember will be required to state the existence of the potential conflict of interest as specified in Regulation 18705(b).

Thus, we conclude that the Act does not prohibit Councilmember Radford from taking part in the decision on whether to amend the WUI map. Although the Act prohibits Councilmembers Harpootlian, Waldeck, and Spreen from taking part in that decision, the Town may invoke the "legally required participation" exception to form a quorum of councilmembers with respect to that decision, as specified in Regulation 18705.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By: Matthew F. Christy
Counsel, Legal Division

Does the Act prohibit Councilmembers Harpootlian, Waldeck, Spreen, or Radford from 
taking part in the governmental decision on whether to amend the Town's Wildland Urban Interface 
("WUI") map?

The Act does not prohibit Councilmember Radford from taking part in the decision on

Waldeck, and Spreen from taking part in that decision, the Town may invoke the "legally required 
participation" exception to form a quorum of councilmembers with respect to the decision, as 
specified in Regulation 18705.

Section 87 I 00 prohibits a public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise 
using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a 
financial interest. Under Section 87103(b ), an official has a financial interest in a decision within 
the meaning of Section 87 I 00 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material 
financial effect on any real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest worth 
$2,000 or more. 
Councilmembers Harpootlian, Waldeck, and Spreen own their respective primary 
residences, alt of which are currently within the WUI area, and Councilmember Radford owns his 
primary residence, located within 500 feet of the border of the existing WUI area. Based on the 
facts presented, these Councilmembers' only interests implicated by the decision on whether to 
amend the WUI map are their respective real property interests in their residences. 
As an initial matter, you ask about segmenting the five options being presented to the city 
council. While in some cases, "large, complex decisions may be sebrmented into separate decisions 
so that even if an official has a disqualifying interest in one component of the series of decisions" 
(emphasis added) where a decision in which an official has a disqualifying interest is "inextricably 
interrelated" to other decisions, the official will be required to disqualify from all the linked 
decisions. Regulation l 8706(b) provides that "decisions are 'inextricably interrelated' when the 
result of one decision will effectively determine, affirm, nullify, or alter the result of another 
decision." 
Your question presents a single decision, what to do with the existing WUI map. All the 
options to amend the WUI map are "inextricably interrelated" to this single decision.2 The result of 
a decision on one of the options would effectively determine, affirm, nullify, or alter the result of 
the decisions on the other options. Therefore, the decision on whether to amend the WUI map is not 
capable of segmentation. 
1 See Pittman Advice Letter, No. A-14.039 (decisions on whether to include particular properties on the 
housing inventory list of the Housing Element of the city's General Plan were inextricably interrelated because the 
decision to include one property could affect the decision on another); Hildreth Advice Letter, No. A-I 0-031 (decisions 
relating to the boundaries of a proposed community plan that could detennine the inclusion or exclusion of a particular 
property were inextricably interrelated); Campos Advice Letter, No. 1-06-072 (decisions to rezone separate parcels to 
meet a high-density housing requirement applicable to all the parcels that could affect the allotment of the burden 
between those parcels were inextricably interrelated.)  
Foreseeability & Materiality: Councilmembers Harpootlian. Waldeck. and Spreen 
A governmental decision's financial effect on a public official's financial interest is 
presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the interest is a named party in, or the subject of, the 
decision. (Regulation 18701(a).) An official's financial interest is a named party in, or subject of, a 
decision if, among other things, the decision affects a real property interest described in Regulation 
l 8702.2(a)( l )-(6). (Ibid.) 
Regulation I 8702.2(a)(2) provides that the reasonably foreseeable effect of a governmental 
decision on an official's real property interest is material whenever the decision determines the 
parcel's inclusion or exclusion from any boundaries, other than boundaries for elective purposes. 
The City Council will soon consider whether to amend the WUI map, and that decision will 
determine whether Councilmembers Harpootlian, Waldeck, and Spreen's respective real property 
interests in their residences will be included within, or excluded from, the amended WUI area. 
Therefore, the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on these 
Councilmembers' respective real property interests. 
Foreseeability & Materiality: Councilmember Radford 
If a public official's financial interest is not explicitly involved in a governmental decision, 
the financial effect of the decision is reasonably foreseeable if the effect can be recognized as a 
realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. (Regulation 1870l(b).) 
A governmental decision's financial effect is material if the decision affects the value of real 
property located within 500 feet of the property line of the official's real property, unless the 
Commission finds that there are sufficient facts to indicate there will be no reasonably foreseeable 
measureable impact on the official's real property interest. (Regulation l 8702.2(a)( 11 ).) 
Councilmember Radford's residence is outside the existing WUI area, and therefore, his 
residence currently is not subject to the more stringent and costly building requirements imposed on 
parcels within the WU! area. Under any of the four options to amend the WUI map under 
consideration, Councilmember Radford's residence would remain outside the WUI area. Although 
the decision at issue will determine whether parcels within 500 feet of Councilmember Radford's 
residence are included within, or excluded from, the WUI area, the inclusion or exclusion of those 
parcels will not affect Councilmember Radford's residence. Therefore, we find that there are 
sufficient facts to indicate that the decision at issue will not have a reasonably foreseeable 
measureable impact on Councilmember Radford's real property interest in his residence. 
Accordingly, the Act does not prohibit Councilmember Radford from taking part in the decision. 
Public Generally Exception 
Section 87103 prohibits an official from taking part in a decision only if the effect of the 
decision on the official's interest is distinguishable from the effect on the public generally. 
Commonly referred to as the .. public generally" exception, Regulation 18703(a) sets forth the 
general rule:  
"A governmental decision's financial effect on a public official's financial 
interest is indistinguishable from its effect on the public generally if the 
official establishes that a significant segment of the public is affected and the 
effect on his or her financial interest is not unique compared to the effect on 
the significant segment." 
For purposes of the "public generally" exception, a "significant segment of the public" 
consists of at least 25 percent of ( 1) the businesses or non-profit entities; (2) the real property, 
commercial real property, or residential real property; or (3) the individuals within the official's 
jurisdiction. (Regulation l 8703(b).) With respect to the decision at issue, the "significant segment 
of the public" is 25 percent of the residential real property within the Town. 
In making the determination on whether to amend the WUI map, the City Council will 
consider four options for its potential amendment. Option 1, to eliminate the WUI map in its 
entirety, would affect 77 percent of all residential parcels in Town, and Option 3, to move the WUI 
boundary back beyond Interstate 280, would affect 33 percent of all residential parcels. However, 
Option 2, to move the boundary back to Interstate 280, would only affect less than 20 percent of all 
residential parcels in Town, and Option 4, to move the boundary generally back to Interstate 280 
but include additional properties in the northernmost portion of Town, would affect less than 17 
percent of all residential parcels. Therefore, the "public generally" exception does not apply 
because the decision on whether to amend the WUI map may not affect a "significant segment of 
the public." 
Accordingly, the Act prohibits Councilmembers Harpootlian, Waldeck, and Spreen from 
taking part in the decision at issue because that decision could have a reasonably foreseeable 
material financial effect on their respective real property interests in their residences distinguishable 
from its effect on the public generally. 
Legally Required Participation Exception 
Under the "legally required participation" exception set forth in Section 87 l 01 and 
Regulation 18705, a public official may make or participate in the making of a governmental 
decision despite being financially interested in the decision if the official's participation is legally 
required for the action or decision to be made. 
Regulation 18705(c} requires the "legally required participation" exception to be construed 
narrowly. The exception cannot be invoked to allow a financially interested official to participate in 
a decision if a quorum3 can be convened of other members of the agency who are not disqualified, 
and participation is required to be limited to the smallest number of officials with a disqualifying 
interest that are .. legally required" to make the decision. (Regulation l 8705(c}(2)-(3 ).) 
Because the Town's City Council is made up of five councilmembers, at least three will be 
necessary to form a quorum to decide whether to amend the WUI map. Councilmembers 
Harpootlian, Waldeck, and Spreen are disqualified from taking part in the decision pursuant to

Section 87100, leaving less than a quorum of councilmembers available to make that determination. 
Therefore, one of those disqualified Councilmembers will be "legally required" to take part in that 
decision in order to conduct business, and the Town may invoke the exception. 
Accordingly, the Town is authorized to use a random means of selection to select a single 
councilmember from among Councilmembers Harpootlian, Waldeck, and Spreen to fonn a quorum 
with respect to the decision at issue. (See Regulation l 8705(c)(3).) When one of those 
council members is selected, that council member is selected for the duration of the proceedings in 
all related matters until that councilmember's participation is no longer legally required, or the need 
for invoking the exception no longer exists. (Ibid.) The selected councilmember will be required to 
state the existence of the potential conflict of interest as specified in Regulation l 8705(b ). 
Thus, we conclude that the Act does not prohibit Councilmember Radford from taking part 
in the decision on whether to amend the WUI map. Although the Act prohibits Councilmembers 
Harpootlian, Waldeck, and Spreen from taking part in that decision, the Town may invoke the 
"legally required participation" exception to form a quorum of councilmembers with respect to that 
decision, as specified in Regulation 18705.

taking part in the governmental decision on whether to amend the Town's Wildland Urban Interface 
("WUI") map?

CONCLUSION: The Act does not prohibit Councilmember Radford from taking part in the decision on 
whether to amend the WUI map. Although the Act prohibits Councilmembers Harpootlian, 
Waldeck, and Spreen from taking part in that decision, the Town may invoke the "legally required 
participation" exception to form a quorum of councilmembers with respect to the decision, as 
specified in Regulation 18705.
